core_question: Why can pension de-risking transform solvency risk into liquidity risk?
intended_thesis: De-risking through leveraged LDI strategies reduces measured funding-ratio volatility, but the leverage and collateral mechanics it relies on concentrate liquidity fragility — so that a rapid rate repricing converts a solvency-hedging structure into a source of systemic liquidity stress.
hard_exclusions: Do not generalize to all mature pension markets without explicit qualifiers.
Do not give direct China policy prescriptions.
Do not claim fund-level causal estimation without fund-level data.
Do not treat this single event as proof of a universal mechanism.
seed_claims: UK LDI strategies created margin-call liquidity pressure during rapid gilt yield movements.
Pension de-risking may reduce funding volatility while increasing liquidity fragility.
Solvency/funding risk and liquidity risk are distinct risk categories that LDI links together.
Leverage and collateral mechanics connect gilt yield movements to forced liquidity demand.
The Bank of England intervention was aimed at market functioning, not pension solvency.
Regulatory frameworks measured solvency risk but did not constrain liquidity mismatch in LDI structures.
known_sources: Bank of England Financial Stability Report, November 2022
Bank of England explanatory material on the September 2022 gilt market intervention
The Pensions Regulator statements on LDI resilience (2022–2023)
Academic and policy commentary on the LDI crisis (author's collected notes)
success_criteria: A frozen question→thesis spine in which every edge is strong or explicitly conditional.
Every empirical claim on the spine bound to at least one archived EvidenceUnit.
The solvency-vs-liquidity conceptual distinction present as an explicit node.
Alternative explanations (e.g. "this was just a rates shock, not a de-risking problem") handled or parked with reasons.
All stated exclusions respected in the final audited prose.
